name: C++ Basics

questions.explanation:
When both operands are `int`, C++ performs **integer division** â€” the fractional part is discarded (truncated toward zero):

```cpp
int x = 10, y = 3;
std::cout << x / y;         // 3  (integer division)
std::cout << 10.0 / 3;      // 3.333...  (floating-point division)
std::cout << (double)x / y; // 3.333...  (explicit cast)
```

choices.text: Bitwise AND â€” combines bits of two operands
Pass by reference â€” `value` is an alias for the caller's variable
Address-of â€” returns the memory address of the argument
Pass by pointer â€” the function receives a pointer to `n`

questions.explanation:
`push_back()` appends an element to the end of the vector, growing its size by 1:

```cpp
std::vector<int> v = {10, 20, 30};  // size = 3
v.push_back(40);                     // size = 4
std::cout << v.size();               // 4
// v is now {10, 20, 30, 40}
```

questions.text: Which of the following correctly declares a **pointer** and assigns it the address of `x`?
questions.explanation:
`int* ptr` declares a pointer to `int`. `&x` takes the **address** of `x`. `*ptr` later **dereferences** the pointer to access the value:

```cpp
int x = 42;
int* ptr = &x;        // ptr holds the address of x

std::cout << ptr;     // memory address, e.g. 0x7ffd...
std::cout << *ptr;    // 42  (dereference)

*ptr = 100;           // changes x through the pointer
std::cout << x;       // 100
```

questions.text:
What does `const` guarantee in this declaration?

```cpp
const int MAX_SIZE = 100;
```

choices.text: The variable is stored in read-only memory (ROM)
The variable's value cannot be changed after initialisation
The variable is only visible within the current function
The variable can be changed exactly once

questions.text:
Which of the following are **valid ways to initialise** a variable in modern C++? (Select all that apply)

```cpp
int a = 5;    // A
int b(5);     // B
int c{5};     // C
auto d = 5;   // D
```
questions.explanation:
All four are valid in modern C++:

```cpp
int a = 5;    // copy initialisation (C++98)
int b(5);     // direct initialisation (C++98)
int c{5};     // brace initialisation (C++11) â€” prevents narrowing
auto d = 5;   // type deduced as int (C++11)

// Brace initialisation prevents accidental narrowing:
int x{3.14};  // error: narrowing from double to int
int y = 3.14; // compiles but silently truncates to 3
```

Prefer `{}` (brace initialisation) in modern C++ â€” it catches type narrowing at compile time.
